reasoning: The base rate of consecutive >18% annual housing price increases in a major city is historically low, suggesting an eventual cooling or reversion to lower growth rates. In December 2024, the average price of housing in Madrid capital reached a historical high of 4,952 euros/m². To resolve YES, the price must exceed 5,843 euros/m² by December 2025. While most initial 2025 forecasts (such as those from Caixabank and various experts) pointed to a national or regional growth of around 5-7%, recent summer 2025 news reports indicate that prices in Spain have continued to surge (e.g., up 14% year-over-year overall by July 2025, with Madrid seeing particularly high increases). Despite these strong interim momentum indicators, achieving >18% growth within a single calendar year—especially off a highly elevated base and following a prior 20% increase—remains difficult and risks affordability constraints. The community prediction from late July 2025 sat at a well-calibrated 25%, factoring in both the unexpectedly strong early-2025 data and the significant hurdle rate. I align with 25% due to the strong recent momentum but inherently low base rate for back-to-back ~20% growth.
